skill.name: writing-hookify-rules
skill.description: Guides users in writing Hookify markdown rules with YAML frontmatter, event matching, conditions, messages, and rule organization.
skill.summary: Create Hookify rules that match prompts, file edits, bash commands, and completion events.
skill.author: CaptainCrouton89

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many dangerous strings, but most are documentation examples for patterns that Hookify rules can detect. The real concern is the documented command interpolation feature, which can execute shell commands from rule message bodies.

medium_findings.title: Command Interpolation Can Execute Shell Commands
medium_findings.description: The skill documents a Hookify feature where message text can include shell commands that are executed and replaced with output. This is legitimate functionality, but copied or untrusted rules could run commands in the user environment.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The documentation explicitly says commands in messages get executed and shows a command interpolation example. The skill itself is documentation-only, so this is a contextual risk rather than confirmed malware.

low_findings.title: Dangerous Command Strings Are Detection Examples
low_findings.description: The destructive command strings are presented as regex patterns for warning rules. I found no evidence that this skill runs rm, dd, mkfs, chmod, or chown commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged strings appear inside examples and reference lists for rule matching. They are not executable code in the skill package.

low_findings.title: Secret and Environment References Are Pattern Examples
low_findings.description: API key, environment file, credential, and certificate references are used as examples for detecting sensitive edits. I found no evidence that the skill reads secrets or environment variables.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The references are inside markdown examples that teach users how to warn about sensitive content. No code path accesses environment files or secret values.

low_findings.title: Hidden Rule Paths Are Expected Configuration Locations
low_findings.description: The hidden file paths describe where Hookify rules are stored for global or project use. This is file organization guidance, not hidden persistence or unauthorized file access.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines describe documented configuration paths and gitignore advice. No filesystem operation is performed by the skill itself.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell Command Interpolation Syntax
dangerous_patterns.description: The documented syntax allows shell commands inside message bodies. Users should only add commands they trust and should avoid commands that expose secrets.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command execution behavior is stated directly in the documentation. The example command appears benign, but the feature is powerful.

content.value_statement: Hookify rules can be hard to write correctly without knowing the event and condition format. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through clear rule files.
actual_capabilities: Explains the required YAML frontmatter for Hookify rule files.
Shows how to match bash, file, stop, prompt, and all events.
Defines simple pattern rules and multi-condition rules.
Gives regex guidance for common rule matching needs.
Documents global and project rule file naming conventions.
Describes command interpolation in message bodies.
limitations: It does not install or run Hookify.
It does not validate rule files automatically.
It does not provide a complete Hookify runtime reference.
Command interpolation requires careful user review before use.

use_cases.title: Add Project Guardrails
use_cases.description: Create rules that warn about risky commands, debug code, or sensitive file edits during development.

use_cases.title: Standardize Team Checks
use_cases.description: Define shared reminders for tests, deployments, reviews, and release steps in a consistent rule format.

use_cases.title: Build Personal Workflow Rules
use_cases.description: Write local Hookify rules that prompt useful reminders across projects without changing repository files.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Rule
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Hookify rule that warns when a bash command includes a dangerous delete pattern.

prompt_templates.title: Match File Edits
prompt_templates.prompt: Write a Hookify rule that detects console logging added to TypeScript files and suggests safer logging.

prompt_templates.title: Use Multiple Conditions
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Hookify rule with conditions for file path and new text. Warn when secrets appear in environment files.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Rule Set
prompt_templates.prompt: Design three Hookify rules for a production deployment workflow, including prompts, bash checks, and stop reminders.

output_examples.input: A request for a rule that warns about production deploy prompts.
output_examples.output: A named prompt-event rule with a clear match condition and a deployment checklist message.

output_examples.input: A request to block unsafe file edits in a project.
output_examples.output: A project rule recommendation with file path conditions, sensitive text matching, and a warning message.

output_examples.input: A request to organize local and shared Hookify rules.
output_examples.output: Guidance on global versus project rule locations, naming, and gitignore handling.

best_practices: Use descriptive rule names that explain the behavior being checked.
Prefer narrow patterns and multiple conditions for high-signal rules.
Review command interpolation carefully before adding it to a rule.
anti_patterns: Do not use broad patterns that match unrelated words or paths.
Do not store project-local rule files without a matching gitignore entry.
Do not run shell commands in messages unless the command is trusted.

faq.question: What does this skill help create?
faq.answer: It helps create Hookify markdown rules with YAML frontmatter, match patterns, conditions, and user-facing messages.

faq.question: Which Hookify events are covered?
faq.answer: It covers bash, file, stop, prompt, and all events.

faq.question: Can it create rules with multiple conditions?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains condition fields, operators, and how all conditions must match.

faq.question: Does it run the rules?
faq.answer: No. It provides writing guidance and examples, but it does not install or execute Hookify rules.

faq.question: Why is command interpolation risky?
faq.answer: Command interpolation can execute shell commands from message text. Only trusted commands should be used.

faq.question: Where are rule files stored?
faq.answer: Rules can be stored globally under the user Claude directory or locally in a project Claude directory.
